one_line_takeaway: A 507-leaf, 932-paper-derived taxonomy and 4x6 STRIDE-grounded Target x Technique matrix show that three major LLM attack benchmarks (HarmBench, InjecAgent, AgentDojo) share zero overlapping coverage cells and jointly cover at most 25% of the matrix, leaving Service Disruption and Model Internals attacks entirely unbenchmarked despite published attacks in those categories reaching 96% ASR, 46x token amplification, and 100x latency increases.
key_finding: The Target x Technique matrix (507 leaves) shows extreme concentration: Safety & Alignment Bypass accounts for 74.4% of leaves (377/507); Obfuscation (132 leaves) and Persuasion (122 leaves) are the largest technique columns, with 87-95% of their leaves falling under Safety Bypass. In the benchmark coverage audit, the three primary public benchmarks (HarmBench: 18 red-teaming methods, 510 behaviors; InjecAgent: 1,054 cases; AgentDojo: 629 test cases) occupy zero overlapping matrix cells and collectively cover at most 6 of 24 cells (25%; 4/24 = <=17% if partial-coverage cells are excluded). Across all six audited benchmarks (adding AdvBench, JailbreakBench, StrongREJECT, which add no new cells), at least 18 of 24 cells lack any standardized evaluation, including the entire Service Disruption row (27 leaves) and the entire Model Internals column (33 leaves). The paper cites specific published-but-unbenchmarked results to show these gaps are not merely theoretical: on identical AdvBench behaviors, logit suppression (Li et al. 2026b, unbenchmarked) reportedly achieves 96% ASR versus 69% ASR for GCG (benchmarked); Service Disruption attacks achieve 46x token amplification (Kumar et al. 2026, Overthink) and 100x latency increases (Li et al. 2026a, ThinkTrap). Naming fragmentation: GCG appears under 29 distinct surface forms across 376 papers; AutoDAN under 11 forms across 259 papers; 60 taxonomy leaves function as catch-alls absorbing 10+ distinct techniques, with the top catch-all (adversarial-perturbation-on-images) absorbing 126 unique attacks. Temporal analysis: novelty rate spiked to 26.9% in H2 2023 before stabilizing at ~18%; Safety Bypass's share of unique attack groups declined from 92% (H2 2023) to 74% (H1 2026) while System & Tool Hijacking grew from 5% to 16%.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is (1) a 507-leaf taxonomy of inference-time LLM attacks (401 populated leaves from the corpus, 106 author-authored placeholder leaves), built by extracting 6,366 attack mentions from 932 arXiv security papers (H1 2023-H1 2026) via Gemini 3.1 Pro structured extraction and normalizing them to 2,521 unique attack groups; (2) a 4x6 Target x Technique matrix grounded in STRIDE (rows: Safety & Alignment Bypass, System & Tool Hijacking, Information Exfiltration, Service Disruption; columns: Instructional, Persuasion & Deception, Obfuscation, Cross-Modal, Indirect Injection, Model Internals), where each taxonomy leaf is classified by adversarial target and bypassed defense layer; and (3) a benchmark-external coverage audit that maps HarmBench, InjecAgent, and AgentDojo (plus AdvBench, JailbreakBench, StrongREJECT in a sensitivity check) onto this matrix to show which target/technique cells have no standardized public evaluation. The authors explicitly frame the coverage finding itself as not novel/expected, and state the reusable audit framework (taxonomy + matrix + released artifacts) as the primary contribution.
threat_model: The scope is inference-time attacks only -- techniques executed through the model's input interface during deployment, requiring no access to the training pipeline or weights (training-phase attacks such as data poisoning, backdoors, and weight manipulation are explicitly excluded from the taxonomy/matrix, though 414 training-phase attack types were extracted and released separately). Adversarial targets (rows) are grounded in STRIDE: Tampering->Safety & Alignment Bypass, Elevation of Privilege->System & Tool Hijacking, Information Disclosure->Information Exfiltration, Denial of Service->Service Disruption; Spoofing and Repudiation are held not applicable to inference-time attacks. Technique columns represent which defense layer is bypassed: Instructional (instruction-priority defenses), Persuasion & Deception (alignment/RLHF), Obfuscation (input filtering), Cross-Modal (text-only safety), Indirect Injection (trust boundaries between system/user/external data), and Model Internals (architecture/representation-level defenses, partially requiring white-box access).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) Pipeline relies solely on Gemini 3.1 Pro for extraction and classification, creating a possible systematic blind spot invisible without an independent check; mitigated partially by the 94.5% Gemini/Claude Opus 4.6 target-row cross-model agreement and human recall spot-checks, but the benchmark audit itself (manual documentation mapping) is stated as unaffected by this risk. (2) Extraction recall (89.4%) is frequency-dependent -- less-established/novel attacks are more likely to be missed -- so the observed novelty stabilization at ~18% is called an 'apparent rate' rather than a precise measurement. (3) Corpus scope is limited to English arXiv papers from the Promptfoo LLM Security Database, which publishes no formal inclusion criteria; a cross-check against an independent survey (Kim et al. 2026) found only 8 of 26 in-scope attack papers (30.8%) present in the corpus directly, though the authors argue the underlying attack techniques from all 18 missing papers are still represented via citing papers already in-corpus. (4) The corpus is weighted toward recent publications (305 papers in H1 2026 vs. 3 in H1 2023), potentially over-representing current trends, though the authors argue this does not affect the benchmark audit specifically. (5) Only six public benchmarks were audited; proprietary/custom red-teaming suites could cover additional cells, so the coverage-gap figures are explicitly presented as a lower bound on the gap (i.e., an upper bound on true coverage). (6) The 4x6 matrix design choice (mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive over the corpus) is acknowledged as one of several possible decompositions.

datasets_used: Promptfoo LLM Security Database (source corpus of 932 arXiv papers, H1 2023-H1 2026)
Authors' own extracted/released dataset: 6,366 attack mentions across 863 papers, normalized to 2,521 unique attack groups, organized into a 507-leaf taxonomy
HarmBench (510 behaviors, 18 red-teaming methods) -- audited benchmark, not a training/eval dataset the authors built
AdvBench, JailbreakBench, StrongREJECT -- audited in sensitivity analysis, added no new matrix cells
quantitative_results: 507-leaf taxonomy: 401 leaves populated by corpus data, 106 author-added placeholder leaves
2,521 unique attack groups from 6,366 raw mentions (60.4% deduplication reduction)
Classification tiers: Tier 1 direct lookup 2.1%, Tier 2 fuzzy match 2.7%, Tier 3 semantic match 95.2%
Human eval of 200 Tier-3 classifications: 92.0% accuracy (95% CI [87.4%, 95.0%])
Inter-annotator agreement (115 double-coded samples): 86.1% at matrix-cell level, leaf-level kappa=0.70
Cross-model (Claude Opus 4.6) re-classification of same 200 attacks: 94.5% target-row agreement, 80.5% matrix-cell agreement
Extraction recall spot-check: 100% precision (76/76), 89.4% recall (76/85)
Safety & Alignment Bypass = 74.4% of all taxonomy leaves (377/507) and 83.8-84% of mapped mentions
Three primary benchmarks (HarmBench, InjecAgent, AgentDojo): zero overlapping cells, cover at most 6/24 cells (25%); drops to 4/24 (<=17%) excluding partial-coverage cells
Across all 6 audited benchmarks: at least 18 of 24 matrix cells uncovered; Service Disruption row (27 leaves) and Model Internals column (33 leaves) entirely uncovered
GCG: 29 distinct surface names across 376 papers; AutoDAN: 11 distinct surface names across 259 papers
60 taxonomy leaves are 'catch-alls' with 10+ absorbed techniques; top catch-all (adversarial-perturbation-on-images) absorbs 126 unique attacks
Novelty rate: spiked to 26.9% in H2 2023, stabilized at ~18% thereafter
Safety Bypass share of unique attack groups fell from 92% (H2 2023) to 74% (H1 2026); System & Tool Hijacking rose from 5% to 16%
Logit suppression attack (unbenchmarked, Li et al. 2026b): 96% ASR vs. GCG (benchmarked): 69% ASR on identical AdvBench behaviors
Overthink attack (Kumar et al. 2026): 46x token amplification (Service Disruption, uncovered by benchmarks)
ThinkTrap attack (Li et al. 2026a): 100x latency increase (Service Disruption, uncovered by benchmarks)
Category novelty rates: Jailbreaking 21.9%, Prompt Injection 31.4%, Info. Disclosure 39.6%, Decoding Manip. 41.5%, Service Disruption 34.3%, Attack Generation 11.0%
Corpus temporal skew: 305 papers in H1 2026 vs. 3 papers in H1 2023
Cross-check vs. independent survey (Kim et al. 2026): of 102 cited arXiv papers, 76 outside DB scope, and of 26 in-scope attack papers only 8 (30.8%) appear in the corpus
baselines_compared: HarmBench (Mazeika et al. 2024) -- audited as primary benchmark, covers only Safety & Alignment Bypass across 4 technique columns
InjecAgent (Zhan et al. 2024) -- audited as primary benchmark, covers System & Tool Hijacking / Information Exfiltration via Indirect Injection only
AgentDojo (Debenedetti et al. 2024) -- audited as primary benchmark, same coverage profile as InjecAgent
AdvBench, JailbreakBench (Chao et al. 2024), StrongREJECT (Souly et al. 2024) -- secondary benchmarks in sensitivity analysis, added no new matrix cells
Xu and Parhi 2025 survey (~150 papers, ~40 named attacks) -- related-work comparison, not a technical baseline the paper benchmarks against
Kim et al. 2026 agentic-AI SoK (128 papers, CIA-grounded taxonomy) -- related-work comparison used for corpus representativeness cross-check
